blurb:
At six years old, Karla has figured out exactly who she is, and who she is happens to be a very good girl. She's a petite German Shepherd with a gentle personality and, according to the volunteers, a coat that occasionally has its own agenda. She's going to need someone willing to take her for the occasional grooming run. That's a pretty easy ask for a dog this sweet.

She gets along with other dogs, has experience with kids, and keeps her energy at a steady, manageable pace. She's not going to tear through your house. She's more the type to settle in beside you and just be present. That kind of calm is genuinely hard to find in a shelter dog.

One thing worth knowing: Karla tested positive for heartworm. It sounds scarier than it is. Lewisville Animal Services will cover her full treatment, so you're not walking into a vet bill surprise. She's also fully vaccinated, spayed, microchipped, and flea-treated. She just needs someone to get her through treatment and into a home where she can finally stay.

She's at Lewisville Animal Services on College Parkway. Walk in during visiting hours to meet her, or call ahead if you have questions about the heartworm protocol.
scoring_notes:
• Heartworm-positive senior Shepherd with fully covered treatment is the highest-stakes, highest-reward story in this set — ideal newsletter centerpiece
• Her exceptionally low intake ID number signals the longest shelter stay of any candidate, making featured placement genuinely urgent
• Calm energy, kid experience, and dog compatibility make her the most broadly adoptable dog in the group despite the medical flag

blurb:
Panda Bear is a five-year-old American Shorthair mix who just arrived at the shelter, so the team is still getting to know her. Her bio is thin right now, but a few things are already clear: she came from a home with multiple other animals and handled it fine, she is litter box trained, and at 11 pounds she is a solid, healthy cat.

The fact that she lived alongside a houseful of other pets says something. Cats who do well in busy multi-pet homes tend to be adaptable and relatively easygoing. She is vaccinated, spayed, and microchipped, so the basics are covered.

Because she is brand new, the shelter does not know yet how she does with kids. If that matters for your household, they can help you figure out next steps before you commit. If you want to be the person who gets to discover who this cat actually is, Panda Bear is a pretty good candidate.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy in Lewisville. Walk in during visiting hours to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Proven multi-pet household background makes her a low-risk, high-compatibility pick for busy adopters
• "Brand new — be the person who discovers who she is" angle creates a first-mover emotional pull
• Just arrived means zero urgency on shelter time; she does not yet need a featured spotlight to drive traffic

blurb:
His name is Porkchop, he weighs 12 pounds, and he has decided he is done with the outdoor life. He came in as a street cat, did the math, and apparently concluded that indoor living is the obvious correct choice. He is not wrong.

Porkchop is three years old, fully vaccinated, neutered, and microchipped. He is litter box trained and has been handling shelter life like a cat who knows something better is coming. He does get a little hesitant in new situations, so he would do best in a quieter home where he can decompress on his own schedule. Not a lot of chaos, not a revolving door of strangers. Just a calm place to land.

His history with kids and other pets is unknown since he came in from outside, but the shelter can talk you through what a meet and greet might look like before you commit. If you have a low-key household and want a cat who genuinely earned his retirement, Porkchop is worth a visit.

Lewisville Animal Services is on College Pkwy in Lewisville. Walk in during visiting hours and ask for Porkchop.
scoring_notes:
• Street-cat-turned-indoor-convert narrative is instantly relatable and gives readers a clear personality hook
• Quiet-home requirement narrows the pool but speaks directly to a highly motivated adopter segment
• Tone implies a settled shelter resident who has been waiting long enough to earn the "retirement" framing

blurb:
Victor is a five-year-old Bulldog mix who wags with his entire body when it's his turn to hang out with the volunteers. Not just his tail. His whole body. That tells you pretty much everything you need to know about this guy.

He came through the prison training program and learned a lot. The volunteers call him a super sweet boy, and bath time apparently turns him into a total angel, calm, relaxed, just soaking up the one-on-one attention. He's energetic and playful, and when he gets excited he can get a little mouthy, not mean, just enthusiastic. He's easy to redirect and the trainers will show you exactly what works with him before you take him home.

Victor would do best as your only dog since he's not a fan of other pups. He's got some strength to him, so older teens and adults are a better fit than young kids. If you're active and want a dog who's going to match your energy and then curl up next to you at the end of the day, he's worth a visit.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Head to their website to learn more about Victor and get the adoption process started.
shelter_hours: See mostlymutts.org for current hours and appointment info
scoring_notes:
• Prison program alumni with full-body wag and bath-time calm is a compelling portrait of a dog who just needs the right active home
• 'Wags with his whole body' is the kind of vivid, specific detail that stops a reader mid-scroll and builds immediate emotional connection
• Solo-dog requirement and adult-only household preference narrow the pool considerably, reinforcing the case for newsletter visibility

blurb:
Shellie is a Husky-Basset Hound mix who somehow managed to inherit the best parts of both breeds, the soulful eyes, the stubborn sweetness, and enough personality to fill a room. She's five years old, about 32 pounds, and according to the Mostly Mutts photographer, she spent her entire photo shoot trying to climb into someone's lap. That tracks.

She's affectionate, cuddly, and kiss-happy. Her foster described her as loving but a little mouthy, in a puppy way, not a concerning way. She came up through the prison training program and has been working one-on-one with a trainer on some food and toy possessiveness. Real progress has been made, and her trainer will walk you through exactly how to keep that momentum going at home. It's manageable stuff, just needs consistency.

Shellie does best without cats and prefers male dogs over females, so if you've already got a laid-back boy dog at home, bring him in for a meet and greet. Kids 15 and up who follow her simple ground rules are a great fit. A fenced yard would make her very happy.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw, and you can reach out through their website to start the process. They'll set you up with everything you need to know about Shellie before you come in.
shelter_hours: See mostlymutts.org for current hours and appointment info
scoring_notes:
• Prison training program background plus active behavioral work signals a longer shelter stay and real placement urgency
• Husky-Basset mix who tried to sit in the photographer's lap is an irresistible visual story with strong newsletter shareability
• Specific household requirements (no cats, male dog preferred, 15+ kids) make her a targeted but powerful appeal to the right reader

blurb:
Lexington goes by Wicket at home, which should tell you something about his personality right away. He's a three-year-old Jack Russell-Rat Terrier mix, about 17 pounds, and he is fully convinced he should be sitting in your lap at all times. He will try. He will succeed.

His foster describes him as a total cuddler with a silly streak and a great balance of playful and chill. He hasn't touched the furniture (a genuine win), loves people, and will position himself as close to you as physically possible the moment you sit down. He was adopted for about eight months before coming back to Mostly Mutts, and it just came down to a bad fit with the cats in that home. Nothing more complicated than that.

Lexington does have a strong prey drive, so no cats. He can warm up to other dogs with a slow, proper intro, and he's done well with mellow pups in his foster home. He's more comfortable with older kids who give him his space, 10 and up at minimum. He's a small dog with a big personality and he's going to be deeply loyal to whoever takes him home.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. When you reach out, ask about Lexington (not Wicket) since that's the name in his records.
shelter_hours: See mostlymutts.org for current hours and appointment info
scoring_notes:
• Returned after eight months through no behavioral fault of his own — a genuinely urgent second-chance story that motivates action
• Dual identity as Lexington and 'Wicket' plus the lap-obsessed cuddler detail gives the newsletter a personality-driven, immediately lovable hook
• Prior adoption return combined with cat and multi-dog restrictions suggests extended shelter exposure, making him the highest-priority feature candidate

blurb:
Cuban came into Mostly Mutts from animal control a little anxious and not quite sure about people yet. That part is changing fast. The volunteers have been putting in the work, and he's now comfortable being picked up for short stretches. He's not ready to be a lap cat tomorrow, but he's getting there, and the trajectory is good.

What he IS ready for right now is playtime. Put a wand toy in front of him and he goes full hunting mode. He'll also bat a ball around one of those track toys for ages by himself. The playful side of him is already out. The snuggly side is just a little behind it, waiting for the right patient person to coax it out.

He came in as part of the Sandwich litter with his brother. He's about two months old and around 1.5 pounds, still growing. He does great with his brother and is working out the social dynamics with his Kitty Room roommates. A home with a calm, friendly cat could be a good fit, and kids are welcome as long as they're gentle and not in a rush for him to warm up overnight.

Cuban is at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Visit mostlymutts.org or stop by in person to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Requires a patient adopter willing to earn his trust — narrower fit, but deeply rewarding for the right reader
• 'Playful side already out, snuggly side just behind it' is an emotionally resonant narrative arc that invites reader investment
• Animal control intake plus ongoing socialization work suggests a longer road to adoption and a stronger case for spotlight urgency

blurb:
Jupiter has two speeds: full chaos and full snuggle, and she cycles between them constantly. She's about two months old, roughly two pounds, and she treats every toy mouse like it's the most important thing she's ever caught. She will chase it across the room, pounce on it, carry it around in her mouth, and then trip over her own paws doing it. It's a lot.

When she finally runs out of steam, she parks herself in the nearest warm lap and purrs as loud as her tiny body will allow. She grew up with her siblings and also did great with an adult cat in her foster home, so if you already have a cat at home, she'd likely slot right in. She's litter trained and doesn't seem to have any strong dislikes, which tracks for a kitten who is still figuring out what the world even is.

Jupiter came in as part of a litter found underneath a car without their mom. Mostly Mutts got them into foster homes and they're now together at the shelter, ready to find their people. She's part of the Solar System litter, which is genuinely a fun detail.

She's currently living in the Kitty Aquarium at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Stop by to meet her in person or check the website for next steps on adoption.
scoring_notes:
• Litter-trained, cat-friendly kitten with zero red flags makes her an effortless adoption decision
• 'Solar System litter' found under a car is a charming, shareable origin story with built-in newsletter hook
• Recently arrived from foster care with siblings present — low urgency but high feel-good factor

blurb:
Two months old, two pounds, and already completely unbothered by dogs. Mercury was found under a car with his siblings, no mom in sight, and a good samaritan made sure they didn't stay there long. He spent time in a foster home and apparently just walked up to the family dog at the gate and shrugged. That's the kind of kitten he is.

His foster described him as a robust little guy who's all about exploring. He loves being held and cuddled, but he's also perfectly happy wandering off to check every corner of the room on his own schedule. He's not clingy and he's not standoffish. He's just confident and curious, which is a great combination in a cat.

He's litter trained, good with other cats, and likely fine with a dog if the dog is cat-friendly. Kids are probably a good fit too, as long as they're gentle and know how to handle something this small. He's part of the Solar System litter at Mostly Mutts, currently living in the Kitty Aquarium at the shelter with his siblings.

Head to Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw to meet him, or visit mostlymutts.org to learn how to get the process started.
scoring_notes:
• Dog-tolerant, kid-friendly, cat-social, and litter-trained — broadest possible adopter pool of any candidate in this batch
• 'Walked up to the family dog and shrugged' is a ready-made viral moment that writes its own headline
• Same recent intake timeline as littermates, but his compatibility range gives him the most urgent case for placement

blurb:
Victor is a five-year-old Bulldog mix, about 44 pounds, and his whole body wags when he's happy. Not just the tail. The whole thing. The volunteers at Mostly Mutts call him a super sweet boy, and he went through the prison training program, so he's had real, consistent work put into him.

He's got a big personality and a lot of love to give. He's calm and angelic at bath time, great with one-on-one attention, and the kind of dog who just wants to be with his person. He can get mouthy when he's excited, which has occasionally turned into an accidental skin grab, but he redirects easily and the trainers will show you exactly what works for him.

He'd do best as your only dog since he hasn't clicked with other dogs. Kids 15 and up are a better fit given his strength and excitement level around younger ones. If that sounds like your house, he's got a lot to offer. Mostly Mutts is in Marietta. Reach out through their website or give them a call to meet Victor.
shelter_hours: Please check mostlymutts.org for current hours
scoring_notes:
• 'Whole body wags' is a quotable, shareable detail that gives a newsletter blurb immediate warmth and viral potential
• Prison training program backstory paired with ongoing mouthiness redirects well as a 'diamond in the rough' narrative
• Only-dog requirement plus age restriction for kids narrows the pool considerably, suggesting he has been waiting and needs a targeted push

blurb:
Shellie is a Husky-Basset mix, and yes, that combination is exactly as charming as it sounds. She's five years old, about 32 pounds, and came to Mostly Mutts from animal control. She's already been through the prison training program and has been working one-on-one with a trainer. She came in a little rough around the edges. She's leaving with some real polish.

The volunteers and her foster family both describe her as loving, cuddly, and full of kisses. She liked being in laps for her photo shoot, which tells you a lot about her. She's got some puppy energy still, a little mouthy in a playful way, and she's genuinely happy to be around her people.

She does have a few things worth knowing. She's possessive of food and toys, and there was one bite related to that. She's been actively working on it with her trainer, who has a whole system in place, and they'll walk you through everything when you meet her. She's not a good fit for cats or female dogs, but she did great with a male dog and an 11-year-old in her foster home. Kids 15 and up who understand the house rules would be a solid match. If you have a male dog at home, bring him by for a meet and greet.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. You can reach out through their website or call to set up a visit and ask about Shellie specifically.
shelter_hours: Please check mostlymutts.org for current hours
scoring_notes:
• Prison training program arc plus active behavioral work with a named trainer is a deeply compelling, newsletter-ready redemption story
• Husky-Basset mix is visually arresting and breed-curiosity alone will drive reader engagement and click-throughs
• Multiple placement considerations — bite history, food guarding, animal restrictions — signal extended shelter stay and real adoption urgency

blurb:
Lexington goes by Wicket around the house, which might be the most fitting nickname for a 17-pound Jack Russell-Rat Terrier mix who answers to neither name consistently and absolutely does not care. He's three years old, currently in a foster home, and by all accounts a total cuddler who will attempt to sit directly in your lap at the earliest opportunity.

His foster family describes him as affectionate, playful, and entertaining in that low-key, keeps-you-laughing kind of way. He's not destructive with furniture, he loves people, and he has a genuinely good balance of active and chill. When you sit down, he's going to want to be right there with you.

He came back to Mostly Mutts after a previous adoption because he couldn't get along with cats, and that's still the case. Strong prey drive, no cats. He can warm up to calm, mellow dogs with a slow introduction, so if you have a laid-back dog at home, a leash walk meet and greet is worth trying. Older kids who give him his space are a better fit. He's also perfectly happy being an only pet if that's what you've got going on.

Mostly Mutts is in Marietta. Inquire about Lexington (not Wicket) through their website or by phone.
shelter_hours: Please check mostlymutts.org for current hours
scoring_notes:
• Returned from a previous adoption — the highest urgency signal in rescue — makes him the strongest candidate for a 'second chance' feature angle
• The Wicket nickname detail and 'answers to neither name' humor give editors a ready-made lighthearted hook that stands out in a newsletter
• Despite the return and cat restriction, his foster-home stability and cuddler reputation make him genuinely placeable with the right reader nudge

blurb:
Mercury is the kind of kitten who will explore every corner of a room and then wander back over and ask to be held. He's about two months old, found under a car with his siblings after their mom went missing. Mostly Mutts got them into foster homes, and now the crew has reunited at the shelter. Mercury is ready to go.

His foster called him a "robust little guy," which tracks. He's outgoing, curious, and not easily rattled. He checked out the dog in his foster home through the gate and just shrugged. He loves being picked up and cuddled, but he's also totally fine doing his own thing when you're busy. Independent without being aloof. He'd probably enjoy another cat at home but isn't needy about it.

He's litter trained and good to go. Mostly Mutts is on Sandy Plains Road in Marietta. Stop in during shelter hours and ask to meet him in the Kitty Aquarium.

Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue
1817 Sandy Plains Rd, Marietta, GA 30066
(770) 924-7000
Wed-Fri 12pm-6pm, Sat-Sun 11am-5pm
scoring_notes:
• Near-perfect adoptability profile — dog-exposure tested, cuddly but independent, zero red flags for a wide range of households
• Shares the sibling rescue backstory but the 'shrugged at the dog' detail gives him a standout personality hook
• Fresh arrival with no wait time signals; best positioned as an easy win for a first-time adopter spotlight

blurb:
Jupiter has two speeds: full chaos and dead asleep on your lap. She's a two-month-old tabby mix who came in as a stray with her siblings, found tucked under a car without their mom. The volunteers scooped them up, got them into foster homes, and now Jupiter is living in the Kitty Aquarium at the shelter, ready to find her people.

Her whole thing is toy mice. She carries them around like she caught them herself, pounces on them from across the room, and occasionally trips over her own paws in the process. Then she crashes, crawls onto the nearest warm human, and purrs at full volume. She did great with an adult cat in her foster home, so if you already have a cat at home, she'll probably fit right in. Dogs are unknown, and kids are welcome as long as they're gentle with a small kitten.

She's litter trained, fully playful, and exactly as ridiculous as a two-pound kitten should be. Mostly Mutts is on Sandy Plains Road in Marietta. You can visit the shelter to meet her in person during adoption hours.

Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue
1817 Sandy Plains Rd, Marietta, GA 30066
(770) 924-7000
Wed-Fri 12pm-6pm, Sat-Sun 11am-5pm
scoring_notes:
• Irresistibly adoptable kitten with playful personality and low behavioral barriers makes her a reader magnet
• Origin story of siblings found under a car without their mom adds just enough heart without being heavy
• Recently arrived via foster pipeline — low urgency, but novelty factor keeps her relevant for a feature

blurb:
Cuban came into Mostly Mutts from animal control with his brother, a little nervous and not sure what to make of any of it. That was then. The volunteers have been putting in the work, and he's opening up more every day. He's not a lap cat yet, but he's getting there, and the right home is going to speed that up a lot.

What he IS right now is a serious little hunter on the play floor. Wand toys, ball tracks, anything that moves fast. Sit down with him and a toy and he'll go hard for as long as you let him. The volunteers can pick him up for short stretches now, and every week he shows a little more of his sweet side. He just needs someone patient who'll meet him where he is.

He's litter trained and does great with his brother. If you want a kitten with a bit of a backstory and room to grow, Cuban is worth going to meet. Mostly Mutts is on Sandy Plains Road in Marietta, open Wednesday through Sunday.

Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue
1817 Sandy Plains Rd, Marietta, GA 30066
(770) 924-7000
Wed-Fri 12pm-6pm, Sat-Sun 11am-5pm
scoring_notes:
• Underdog narrative — came in scared from animal control and is visibly growing — creates the emotional arc readers respond to most
• 'Every week he shows a little more of his sweet side' signals ongoing shelter stay, elevating urgency for a feature
• Patient adopter angle broadens appeal to readers who want a meaningful bond rather than a ready-made pet
